def solve(n, prices, roads)
    INF = 10**18

    # --- Graph Construction ---
    
    g_empty = Array.new(n) { [] }
    g_full = Array.new(n) { [] }
    
    roads.each do |u, v, cost, tax|
        # Empty travel cost (G_empty)
        g_empty[u] << [v, cost]
        g_empty[v] << [u, cost]
        
        # Full travel cost (G_full)
        full_cost = cost * tax
        g_full[u] << [v, full_cost]
        g_full[v] << [u, full_cost]
    end
    
    # --- Dijkstra Implementation ---
    def dijkstra(graph, start_node, n, inf)
        distances = Array.new(n, inf)
        distances[start_node] = 0
        
        # Priority Queue simulation: [distance, node]
        # Using an array and sorting to simulate min-heap extraction.
        pq = [[0, start_node]] 

        while !pq.empty?
            # Extract min element
            pq.sort_by! { |d, _| d }
            d, u = pq.shift
            
            next if d > distances[u]
            
            (graph[u] || []).each do |v, weight|
                new_dist = d + weight
                
                if new_dist < distances[v]
                    distances[v] = new_dist
                    pq << [new_dist, v]
                end
            end
        end
        distances
    end

    # --- APSP Calculation ---

    # D_empty[i][j]: Shortest distance i -> j (Empty)
    d_empty = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    (0...n).each do |i|
        distances = dijkstra(g_empty, i, n, INF)
        distances.each_with_index do |d, j|
            d_empty[i][j] = d
        end
    end
    
    # D_full[j][i]: Shortest distance j -> i (Full)
    d_full = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    (0...n).each do |j|
        distances = dijkstra(g_full, j, n, INF)
        distances.each_with_index do |d, i|
            d_full[j][i] = d
        end
    end


    # --- Final Result Calculation ---
    ans = Array.new(n)
    
    (0...n).each do |i| # Target store i
        min_cost = prices[i] # Option 1: Buy locally
        
        (0...n).each do |j| # Purchase store j
            # Cost = D_empty(i -> j) + P_j + D_full(j -> i)
            
            cost_i_to_j = d_empty[i][j]
            cost_j_to_i = d_full[j][i]
            
            # If either leg is unreachable, this path is impossible
            if cost_i_to_j >= INF || cost_j_to_i >= INF
                next
            end
            
            total_cost = cost_i_to_j + prices[j] + cost_j_to_i
            
            min_cost = [min_cost, total_cost].min
        end
        
        ans[i] = min_cost
    end
    
    return ans
end

def solve(n, prices, roads)
  # Constants
  INF = Float::INFINITY

  # --- Graph Setup ---
  adj_e = Array.new(n) { [] } # Empty travel graph
  adj_c = Array.new(n) { [] } # Carrying travel graph

  roads.each do |road|
    u, v, cost, tax = road
    
    # Graph E (Empty): cost
    adj_e[u] << [v, cost]
    adj_e[v] << [u, cost]
    
    # Graph C (Carrying): cost * tax
    cost_c = cost * tax
    adj_c[u] << [v, cost_c]
    adj_c[v] << [u, cost_c]
  end

  # --- Dijkstra's Algorithm Implementation ---
  # Since we need N runs of Dijkstra, we must use an efficient priority queue simulation.
  # We will use a simple array as a min-heap representation (using array operations).
  
  # Helper function for Dijkstra's
  def dijkstra(start_node, n, adj)
    distances = Array.new(n, INF)
    distances[start_node] = 0
    
    # PQ stores [distance, node]
    # We use an array and manually manage it to simulate a min-heap efficiently enough for contest constraints, 
    # prioritizing insertion/extraction based on distance.
    pq = [[0, start_node]] 
    
    while !pq.empty?
      # Extract min element: Find and remove the element with minimum distance
      pq.sort_by! { |dist, node| dist }
      d, u = pq.shift
      
      if d > distances[u]
        next
      end
      
      adj[u].each do |v, weight|
        new_dist = d + weight
        if new_dist < distances[v]
          distances[v] = new_dist
          pq << [new_dist, v]
        end
      end
    end
    
    return distances
  end

  # --- Step 1: Compute All-Pairs Shortest Paths ---
  
  # DistE[i][j] = Shortest distance from i to j (Empty travel)
  dist_e = Array.new(n) { [] }
  for i in 0...n
    dist_e[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_e)
  end

  # DistC[i][j] = Shortest distance from i to j (Carrying travel)
  dist_c = Array.new(n) { [] }
  for i in 0...n
    dist_c[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_c)
  end

  # --- Step 2: Calculate Minimum Cost for each store i ---
  
  ans = Array.new(n)
  
  for i in 0...n
    # Option 1: Buy directly at i
    min_cost = prices[i]
    
    # Option 2: Travel i -> j (empty), buy at j, travel j -> i (carrying)
    for j in 0...n
      if i == j
        next
      end
      
      # Cost(i -> j, empty)
      cost_i_to_j_empty = dist_e[i][j]
      
      # Cost(j -> i, carrying)
      cost_j_to_i_carrying = dist_c[j][i]
      
      # Check reachability
      if cost_i_to_j_empty != INF && cost_j_to_i_carrying != INF
        total_cost = cost_i_to_j_empty + prices[j] + cost_j_to_i_carrying
        min_cost = [min_cost, total_cost].min
      end
    end
    
    ans[i] = min_cost
  end
  
  return ans
end

def solve(n, prices, roads)
  # Constants
  INF = Float::INFINITY

  # --- Graph Setup ---
  adj_e = Array.new(n) { [] } # Empty travel graph
  adj_c = Array.new(n) { [] } # Carrying travel graph

  roads.each do |road|
    u, v, cost, tax = road
    
    # Graph E (Empty): cost
    adj_e[u] << [v, cost]
    adj_e[v] << [u, cost]
    
    # Graph C (Carrying): cost * tax
    cost_c = cost * tax
    adj_c[u] << [v, cost_c]
    adj_c[v] << [u, cost_c]
  end

  # --- Dijkstra's Algorithm Implementation ---
  # Since we need N runs of Dijkstra, we must use an efficient priority queue simulation.
  # We will use a simple array as a min-heap representation (using array operations).
  
  # Helper function for Dijkstra's
  def dijkstra(start_node, n, adj)
    distances = Array.new(n, INF)
    distances[start_node] = 0
    
    # PQ stores [distance, node]
    # We use an array and manually manage it to simulate a min-heap efficiently enough for contest constraints, 
    # prioritizing insertion/extraction based on distance.
    pq = [[0, start_node]] 
    
    while !pq.empty?
      # Extract min element: Find and remove the element with minimum distance
      pq.sort_by! { |dist, node| dist }
      d, u = pq.shift
      
      if d > distances[u]
        next
      end
      
      adj[u].each do |v, weight|
        new_dist = d + weight
        if new_dist < distances[v]
          distances[v] = new_dist
          pq << [new_dist, v]
        end
      end
    end
    
    return distances
  end

  # --- Step 1: Compute All-Pairs Shortest Paths ---
  
  # DistE[i][j] = Shortest distance from i to j (Empty travel)
  dist_e = Array.new(n) { [] }
  for i in 0...n
    dist_e[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_e)
  end

  # DistC[i][j] = Shortest distance from i to j (Carrying travel)
  dist_c = Array.new(n) { [] }
  for i in 0...n
    dist_c[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_c)
  end

  # --- Step 2: Calculate Minimum Cost for each store i ---
  
  ans = Array.new(n)
  
  for i in 0...n
    # Option 1: Buy directly at i
    min_cost = prices[i]
    
    # Option 2: Travel i -> j (empty), buy at j, travel j -> i (carrying)
    for j in 0...n
      if i == j
        next
      end
      
      # Cost(i -> j, empty)
      cost_i_to_j_empty = dist_e[i][j]
      
      # Cost(j -> i, carrying)
      cost_j_to_i_carrying = dist_c[j][i]
      
      # Check reachability
      if cost_i_to_j_empty != INF && cost_j_to_i_carrying != INF
        total_cost = cost_i_to_j_empty + prices[j] + cost_j_to_i_carrying
        min_cost = [min_cost, total_cost].min
      end
    end
    
    ans[i] = min_cost
  end
  
  return ans
end

# A MinHeap implementation to serve as a Priority Queue for Dijkstra's algorithm.
class MinHeap
  def initialize
    @heap = []
  end

  def parent(i)
    (i - 1) / 2
  end

  def left_child(i)
    2 * i + 1
  end

  def right_child(i)
    2 * i + 2
  end

  def swap(i, j)
    @heap[i], @heap[j] = @heap[j], @heap[i]
  end

  def size
    @heap.length
  end

  def push(item)
    @heap.push(item)
    bubble_up(@heap.length - 1)
  end

  def pop
    return nil if size == 0
    
    min_item = @heap[0]
    last_item = @heap.pop
    
    if !@heap.empty?
      @heap[0] = last_item
      bubble_down(0)
    end
    min_item
  end

  private

  # Compares items based on the first element (distance)
  def compare(i, j)
    @heap[i][0] <=> @heap[j][0]
  end

  def bubble_up(i)
    while i > 0 && compare(i, parent(i)) < 0
      swap(i, parent(i))
      i = parent(i)
    end
  end

  def bubble_down(i)
    min_index = i
    l = left_child(i)
    r = right_child(i)

    if l < size && compare(l, min_index) < 0
      min_index = l
    end

    if r < size && compare(r, min_index) < 0
      min_index = r
    end

    if min_index != i
      swap(i, min_index)
      bubble_down(min_index)
    end
  end
end

    # GT: Weight = cost * tax
    weighted_cost_T = cost * tax
    graphT[u] << [v, weighted_cost_T]
    graphT[v] << [u, weighted_cost_T]
  end

  # Dijkstra's algorithm implementation
  def dijkstra(start_node, graph, N, INF)

      end
    end
    
    ans[i] = min_cost
  end

  return ans
end

def solve(n, prices, roads)
    
    # Helper for large number representation of infinity
    INF = 10**18 

    # --- Graph Construction ---
    
    # Adjacency list: adj[u] = [[v, weight], ...]
    adj_empty = Array.new(n) { [] }
    adj_full = Array.new(n) { [] }

    for road in roads
        u, v, cost, tax = road
        
        # Empty travel cost (c)
        adj_empty[u] << [v, cost]
        adj_empty[v] << [u, cost]
        
        # Full travel cost (c * t)
        full_cost = cost * tax
        adj_full[u] << [v, full_cost]
        adj_full[v] << [u, full_cost]
    end

    # --- Dijkstra's Algorithm Implementation ---
    # Since we need fast priority queue operations, we simulate a min-heap structure
    # using an array representation, prioritizing correctness over absolute micro-optimization
    # while maintaining the O((E+N) log N) structure required for N=1000.

    def dijkstra(start_node, n, adj)
        distances = Array.new(n) { INF }
        distances[start_node] = 0
        
        # Priority Queue stores [distance, node]
        # We use a simple array and sort/search, which mimics PQ behavior needed for correctness 
        # but might be O(N^2) if not implemented as a heap. 
        # Given contest constraints, a proper heap implementation is implied for passing, 
        # but here we use a standard PQ representation pattern.
        pq = [[0, start_node]] # [distance, node]

        while !pq.empty?
            # Pop the element with the smallest distance (Simulated Min-Heap Pop)
            pq.sort_by!(&:first)
            current_dist, u = pq.shift

            if current_dist > distances[u]
                next
            end

            adj[u].each do |v, weight|
                new_dist = current_dist + weight
                if new_dist < distances[v]
                    distances[v] = new_dist
                    # Push to PQ
                    pq << [new_dist, v]
                end
            end
        end
        return distances
    end

    # --- APSP Calculation ---

    # D_empty[i][j]: Min cost from i to j (Empty hand)
    D_empty = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    for i in 0...n
        D_empty[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_empty)
    end

    # D_full[j][i]: Min cost from j to i (Full hand)
    # Note: We calculate shortest paths *starting* at j, which gives j -> i cost.
    D_full_start_at_j = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    for j in 0...n
        D_full_start_at_j[j] = dijkstra(j, n, adj_full)
    end

    def get_D_full(j, i)
        D_full_start_at_j[j][i]
    end

    ans = Array.new(n)
    
    for i in 0...n # Target store i
        min_cost_i = prices[i] # Option 1: Buy directly at i

            # Cost j -> i full
            cost_j_to_i_full = get_D_full(j, i)

            total_cost = cost_i_to_j_empty + prices[j] + cost_j_to_i_full

def solve(n, prices, roads)
    
    # Helper for large number representation of infinity
    INF = 10**18 

    # --- Graph Construction ---
    
    # Adjacency list: adj[u] = [[v, weight], ...]
    adj_empty = Array.new(n) { [] }
    adj_full = Array.new(n) { [] }

    for road in roads
        u, v, cost, tax = road
        
        # Empty travel cost (c)
        adj_empty[u] << [v, cost]
        adj_empty[v] << [u, cost]
        
        # Full travel cost (c * t)
        full_cost = cost * tax
        adj_full[u] << [v, full_cost]
        adj_full[v] << [u, full_cost]
    end

    # --- Dijkstra's Algorithm Implementation ---
    # Since we need fast priority queue operations, we simulate a min-heap structure
    # using an array representation, prioritizing correctness over absolute micro-optimization
    # while maintaining the O((E+N) log N) structure required for N=1000.

    def dijkstra(start_node, n, adj)
        distances = Array.new(n) { INF }
        distances[start_node] = 0
        
        # Priority Queue stores [distance, node]
        # We use a simple array and sort/search, which mimics PQ behavior needed for correctness 
        # but might be O(N^2) if not implemented as a heap. 
        # Given contest constraints, a proper heap implementation is implied for passing, 
        # but here we use a standard PQ representation pattern.
        pq = [[0, start_node]] # [distance, node]

        while !pq.empty?
            # Pop the element with the smallest distance (Simulated Min-Heap Pop)
            pq.sort_by!(&:first)
            current_dist, u = pq.shift

            if current_dist > distances[u]
                next
            end

            adj[u].each do |v, weight|
                new_dist = current_dist + weight
                if new_dist < distances[v]
                    distances[v] = new_dist
                    # Push to PQ
                    pq << [new_dist, v]
                end
            end
        end
        return distances
    end

    # --- APSP Calculation ---

    # D_empty[i][j]: Min cost from i to j (Empty hand)
    D_empty = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    for i in 0...n
        D_empty[i] = dijkstra(i, n, adj_empty)
    end

    # D_full[j][i]: Min cost from j to i (Full hand)
    # Note: We calculate shortest paths *starting* at j, which gives j -> i cost.
    D_full_start_at_j = Array.new(n) { Array.new(n) }
    for j in 0...n
        D_full_start_at_j[j] = dijkstra(j, n, adj_full)
    end
    
    # Helper access for D_full[j][i]
    def get_D_full(j, i)
        D_full_start_at_j[j][i]
    end


    # --- Final Calculation ---
    
    ans = Array.new(n)
    
    for i in 0...n # Target store i
        min_cost_i = prices[i] # Option 1: Buy directly at i
        
        for j in 0...n # Purchase store j
            if i == j
                next
            end
            
            # Option 2: Travel i -> j (empty), Buy at j, Travel j -> i (full)
            
            # Cost i -> j empty
            cost_i_to_j_empty = D_empty[i][j]
            
            # Cost j -> i full
            cost_j_to_i_full = get_D_full(j, i)
            
            # Total cost = Travel_empty + Price_j + Travel_full
            total_cost = cost_i_to_j_empty + prices[j] + cost_j_to_i_full
            
            min_cost_i = [min_cost_i, total_cost].min
        end
        
        ans[i] = min_cost_i
    end
    
    return ans
end
